coredata.dc:title: Receptor-free phenothiazine derivative as fluorescent probe for picric acid: Investigation of the inner filter effect channel
coredata.prism:publicationName: Spectrochimica Acta Part A: Molecular and Biomolecular Spectroscopy
coredata.dc:creator: Kathiravan, Arunkumar
Narayanan, Mahalakshmi
Asha Jhonsi, Mariadoss
Anbazhagan, Venkattappan
coredata.dc:description: In this study, a highly fluorescent and receptor-free phenothiazine derivative (PDAB) was developed to detect picric acid. A combination of steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence studies was conducted to examine the excited state behavior of PDAB with picric acid in solution. The PDAB probe displayed a significant degree of selectivity and was highly sensitive to picric acid, with an extremely low detection limit of 9.82 nM. Time-resolved fluorescence quenching studies exhibit direct evidence of an inner filter effect-based sensing mechanism. Using the Parker equation, a thorough analysis was done to correct the inner filter effect on the sensing of picric acid. Overall, these studies provide critical information on the sensing mechanism for picric acid detection.
coredata.dcterms:subject: Inner filter effect
Phenothiazine
Picric acid
TCSPC
Explosive

A.K. also thanks the sophisticated analytical instruments facility (SAIF), Indian Institute of Technology, Chennai, for the fluorescence lifetime measurements. ce:caption.ce:simple-para: (a) absorption, (b) steady-state and (c) time-resolved fluorescence of PDAB in various solvents. λexi: 390 nm, λemi: ∼480 nm and IRF: 111 ps. (d) a plot of fluorescence lifetime (τ) vs dielectric constant (ε) of the solvents. The concentration of PDAB is 0.5 µM.

(a) Selectivity plot of PDAB in the presence of various nitro aromatics, (b) interference plot of PDAB with anions and (c) cations. λexi: 390 nm. (DNP dinitrophenol, DNT: dinitrotoluene, NP: 4-nitrophenol & NA: 4-nitroaniline).
(a) Fluorimetric titration studies of PDAB in THF with various PA concentrations (b) time-resolved fluorescence decay of PDAB with 2.5 μM PA and (c) LOD plot.

(a) Normalized absorption spectra of PDAB and various nitro aromatics, (b) absorption spectral studies of PDAB with various PA concentrations, (c) A plot of observed and corrected fluorescence intensities of PDAB with various PA concentrations and (d) Efficiency of observed and corrected fluorescence intensities, with removal of IFE.
Synthetic route for fluorescent probe PDAB.

head.ce:title: Receptor-free phenothiazine derivative as fluorescent probe for picric acid: Investigation of the inner filter effect channel

ce:affiliation.ce:source-text: Department of Chemistry, Vinayaka Mission’s Kirupananda Variyar Arts and Science College, Vinayaka Mission’s Research Foundation (Deemed to be University), Salem 636 308, India

ce:abstract-sec.ce:simple-para: In this study, a highly fluorescent and receptor-free phenothiazine derivative (PDAB) was developed to detect picric acid. A combination of steady-state and time-resolved fluorescence studies was conducted to examine the excited state behavior of PDAB with picric acid in solution. The PDAB probe displayed a significant degree of selectivity and was highly sensitive to picric acid, with an extremely low detection limit of 9.82 nM. Time-resolved fluorescence quenching studies exhibit direct evidence of an inner filter effect-based sensing mechanism. Using the Parker equation, a thorough analysis was done to correct the inner filter effect on the sensing of picric acid. Overall, these studies provide critical information on the sensing mechanism for picric acid detection.

ce:data-availability.ce:para: Data will be made available on request.

ce:section.ce:para: Nowadays, there has been an increase in domestic terrorist activity, necessitating the rapid detection of explosives. Explosives are generally made using nitroaromatic organic chemicals, such as nitrobenzene, 2,4,6-trinitrotoluene (TNT), 2,6-dinitrotoluene, and 2,4,6-trinitrophenol or picric acid (PA). PA has greater explosive potential than TNT and was frequently employed as an explosive material in the early years due to its shock-sensitive characteristics[1,2]. PA serves a variety of purposes outside of the military, including the production of fireworks, matches, rocket fuel, pharmaceuticals, and colored dyes[3,4]. PA is poisonous to humans and can cause anemia, liver damage, and lung damage in addition to being explosive[5]. PA contamination is substantially more severe than contamination brought on by other nitroaromatics because PA is extremely soluble in water. The permissible PA content in groundwater is 0.001 mg/L [6]. As a result, trace level of PA detection is an important requirement for reducing environmental pollution. For the detection and quantification of PA and other nitroaromatics, gas chromatography combined with mass spectrometry, gas chromatography-electron capture detection, surface-enhanced Raman spectroscopy, mass spectrometry, X-ray imaging, thermal neutron analysis, electrochemical procedures, and ion mobility spectroscopy (IMS) have all been used or proposed [7–12]. Despite the benefits of all the methods discussed above, none of them is perfect because of drawbacks like lack of portability, sensitivity to false positives caused by ambient pollutants, and false-negative readings caused by particular interfering substances. Thus, there is a pressing need for the development of sensing techniques to provide quick, ease and accurate PA detection.

In recent years, optical detection approaches based on the development of colorimetric and fluorimetric chemosensors have garnered considerable interest [13]. Importantly, the optical technique is particularly appealing due to the vast variety of fluorescent and dye entities [14], the ease of instrumentation demanded when using colorimetric probes [15,16], and the often low detection limit reached when using fluorescent probes [17]. Typically, when compared to absorbance-based approaches, fluorescence-based detection is often one to three orders of magnitude more sensitive and has wider linear ranges. Furthermore, the source and detector of a fluorescence approach might be simply integrated into a handheld device for explosive detection in the field. As a result, the fluorescence-based approach holds considerable promise for use in the rapid, sensitive, and selective detection of explosives [18–21]. Fluorescence-based explosive detection is an indirect technology that employs fluorescent sensory materials that undergo fluorescence intensity changes upon interaction with explosives (non-fluorescent). In theory, any phenomenon that causes a change in fluorescence intensity (quenching or enhancement) and wavelength (bathochromic or hypsochromic shift) has the potential to detect non-fluorescent explosives. The majority of the research that has been published so far on fluorescence-based explosives sensors has focused on fluorescence quenching approaches [22,23]. The quenching of fluorescence can be caused by a number of various processes [24–26], including the photoinduced electron transfer (PET), the Forster resonance energy transfer (FRET), the inner filter effect (IFE), etc. Notably, all of the mechanisms (except IFE) discussed above involve chemical interactions between the probe and the analytes. As a result, an additional receptor is required for these interactions.

In recent years, a novel sensitive IFE-based fluorescent approach has been developed for chemical and biological sensing [27–29], despite the fact that the IFE is a prevalent issue in fluorescence spectroscopy [30,31]. The general IFE sensing strategy is that the absorber's absorption band greatly overlaps the fluorophore's excitation region, allowing the absorber to be detected by a quenching in fluorescence intensity. In this case, the excited state fluorescence lifetime won’t be altered. Moreover, the IFE sensing strategy [32]does not require any kind of chemical link between the molecules of the fluorophore and the quencher. As a result, IFE has recently attracted a lot of interest in the field of fluorimetric sensors [33–36] because of its ease of use, adaptability, and significantly improved sensitivity/selectivity. However, less focus has been given to the development of IFE-based fluorescent probes for the detection of analytes including nitroaromatics. In light of this, the development of an alternative approach for detecting nitroaromatics that overcomes the limits of the present methods continues to be a problem for researchers. The two-step design and synthesis of the phenothiazine derivative (PDAB) (Scheme 1 ) is reported in this context. Picric acid was able to effectively quenching the fluorescence of PDAB, due to sufficient spectral overlap. The quenching mechanisms were determined to be static quenching and the inner filter effect between PDAB and picric acid. The results of the spectral overlap and time-resolved fluorescence provide strong support for the IFE quenching, and furthermore, the fluorescence intensity quenching data is directly utilized to calculate the detection limit of the PDAB.

The required starting materials and reagents for this work were procured from commercial sources (Sigma-Aldrich and Avra chemicals, India). Column chromatography was carried out using silica gel (100–120 mesh) and LR grade solvents. The thin layer chromatography (TLC) was conducted on pre-coated Merck silica gel F254 plates (0.25 mm). The high-performance liquid chromatography (HPLC) grade solvents were used for photophysical and sensing investigations.

Nuclear magnetic resonance (NMR) spectroscopy was performed on a BRUKER spectrometer operating at 500 MHz for 1H NMR and the spectrum collected at ambient probe temperature calibrated to residual solvent. Liquid chromatography–mass spectrophotometer (Make: SPINCOTECH PVT LTD) was utilized for recording mass spectrum. The fluorescent sensor probe (PDAB) was prepared from phenothiazine in two synthetic steps. As a first step, the phenothiazine was ethylated using EtBr/NaOH at room temperature followed by formylation with POCl3/DMF. The formylated intermediate was then exposed to 1,2 diaminobenzene to obtain the desired probe PDAB. The characterization data were presented in supporting information as figures S1-S3.

The photophysical and sensing studies were investigated using Shimadzu UV-1800 spectrophotometer and Hitachi F-4500 fluorescence spectrometer. The Fluorocube - Lifetime System (JOBIN-VYON) has been employed to measure the fluorescence decay of PDAB in various solvents. The fluorescence quantum yield (ϕ F) measurements of PDAB in various solvents were measured using quinine sulphate (ϕ R = 0.54) as a standard[37]. Fluorescence titration method was adapted for selective sensing of picric acid in the concentration range of 0.03 μM – 2.5 μM.

As synthesized PDAB is copiously soluble in variety of organic solvents (DMSO, DMF, MeOH, ACN and THF). Hence, solvatochromic analysis was performed to comprehend ground and excited state behaviour before being applied to sensing applications. The normalized absorption and fluorescence spectra of PDAB in various solvents are depicted in Fig. 1
a&b. Bathochromic shift was observed in the fluorescence wavelength with respect to the polarity of the solvents, indicating that positive solvatochromism exists in PDAB. The positive solvatochromism [38,39] directly confirms the intramolecular charge transfer transition in PDAB (phenothiazine to benzimidazole moiety) upon photoexcitation. To ascertain this, we have conducted time-resolved fluorescence decay measurements (Fig. 1 c) with the above-mentioned solvents. All the decays were fitted with single exponential function [F(t) = Aexp(−t/τ)] with chi-square (χ2) between 0.95 and 1.1 (Figure S4). Intriguingly, the fluorescence lifetime of PDAB is highly dependent on the dielectric constant of the solvents [40]. Furthermore, the plot of fluorescence lifetime vs dielectric constant (Fig. 1 d) of the solvents revealed a linear relationship, indicating that the positive solvatochromism. However, the absorption spectrum of PDAB altered with the H-abstraction [41] or donating solvents (DMSO, DMF & MeOH). As a result, the PDAB molecule is ideal for use as a fluorescent probe since the solvent environment affects the ground state due to the presence of the benzimidazole moiety. For sensing work, we have selected the THF solvent due to its excellent solvency and it is not associated with any type of H-bonding with PDAB molecule [42]. THF and ACN have similar properties; however, considering toxicity of ACN, we chose THF for sensing studies. Notable fluorescence parameters such as fluorescence quantum yield and fluorescence lifetime are critical in sensor applications. The measured fluorescence quantum yield of PDAB in THF solvent is ∼ 30%, making it suitable for use as a fluorescent probe. The fluorescence decay of PDAB was nicely fitted with a single exponential function, and the determined lifetime is 5.48 ns [43]. Various photophysical processes, including internal conversion (IC) and intersystem crossing (ISC), diminish the fluorescence lifetime of PDAB and indirectly decrease the fluorescence quantum yield. Therefore, using the Strickler-Berg equation [44], the natural lifetime (τN = τ/ϕ) was calculated to be 18.26 ns. The calculated natural lifetime of PDAB is indeed sufficient for fluorescence-based sensing measurements.

The sensing of nitro aromatics was investigated utilizing the fluorescence titration method by taking advantage of the strong fluorescence characteristics of PDAB. As a first step, the selective detection of nitro aromatics was performed using the fluorescence quenching response of several nitro compounds added individually to the PDAB solution. According to the Fig. 2
a, the fluorescence quenching efficiency of picric acid (PA) was higher, whereas the presence of the other nitro aromatics had no effect on the quenching efficiency. This clearly demonstrates that PDAB is selective in its sensing of PA. Furthermore, the interference effect of different anions and cations on the selectivity of PDAB towards PA sensing was investigated. Accordingly, the fluorescence spectra of PDAB with PA (3 μM) in the presence of interferences (3 μM) were investigated. The fluorescence quenching efficiency of PDAB/PA was not significantly affected by other interferences such as anions and cations, as shown in Fig. 2 b&c. This observation confirms a negligible interference effect on PA sensing by PDAB. The fluorescence titration method was used to quantify the PA using fluorescent PDAB. Fig. 3
a shows the fluorescence spectrum of PDAB with varying the PA concentrations. Without PA, the fluorescence spectrum of PDAB shows a strong and peak maximum at 467 nm, which gradually decreases with increasing PA concentrations. It is important to note that the concentrations of PA did not affect the shape of the fluorescence spectrum of PDAB. This clearly implies that there is no formation of excited state complexes, dimers and so on. The type of fluorescence quenching of PDAB by PA is analyzed by the typical Stern-Volmer equation (I0/I = 1 + KSV[Q]). Here, I0 and I is the fluorescence intensity of PDAB and with PA, respectively and KSV is Stern-Vomer quenching constant. The experimental Stern-Volmer plot exhibited nonlinear response, indicating that static quenching was dominant. Time-resolved fluorescence decay measurements were performed to provide direct evidence for static quenching, and this measurement is widely used to classify the type of quenching. Fig. 3 b depicts the fluorescence decay of PDAB in the presence of the highest concentration of PA. The fluorescence lifetime of PDAB with PA does not change, confirming the existence of static type quenching. The limit of detection (LOD) was calculated from the fluorescence quenching data by using equation 3 σ/K, where σ is the standard deviation of the fluorescence intensity, and K is the slope of the linear plot. Fig. 3 c shows the LOD curve and from the linear portion, the LOD is calculated to be 9.82 nM. This high sensitivity value indicates that the PDAB probe is effective in sensing PA at trace levels.

In general, many quenching pathways, such as Förster resonant energy transfer (FRET), photoinduced electron transfer (PET), aggregation caused quenching (ACQ), and inner filter effect (IFE), which can deteriorate the fluorescence. To better understand the PDAB quenching pathways with PA, corresponding analyses were performed on each of these effects. To begin, the PA has a broad absorption spectrum ranging from 300 nm to 450 nm, which nearly overlaps with the absorption spectrum of PDAB (Fig. 4
a). This clearly indicates that FRET or IFE may have occurred during the quenching process. The FRET necessitates the optimal distance between the PDAB and the PA in order to accomplish a viable energy transfer, which is only possible if they interact in some way. Fluorescence lifetime studies were carried out to determine the involvement of FRET. The PDAB fluorescence lifetime was measured before and after the addition of PA to the PDAB solution. The lifetime of PDAB remains unchanged even after the addition of PA to their respective solutions, as shown in Fig. 3 b. This confirms that fluorescence quenching is static in nature and rules out the possibility of FRET and PET being involved in the mechanism. In the interest of better comprehending the role of static quenching in the mechanism, the absorption spectra of PDAB in the presence of PA were recorded (Fig. 4 b). The absorption band of PDAB after the addition of PA completely overlapped with the PDAB, and only a hyperchromic effect with no new peak appearance or any shift in the spectrum was observed. This means that the interaction between PDAB and PA is very weak, and no ground state complexation or aggregation appears upon addition of PA. As a result of this observation, the static quenching mechanism and ACQ in the sensing process are ruled out. The aforementioned research findings point to the presence of IFE in the sensing process. Furthermore, the IFE is supported by Fig. 4 a, where efficient overlap of the both PDAB and PA absorption spectrum which results in a strong IFE, whereas inefficient overlap with other nitroaromatics results in a poor IFE, leading to a greater selectivity of PDAB towards PA.

To demonstrate the role of the IFE [45] in the suppression of PDAB fluorescence, the observed fluorescence intensity was corrected using the equation (1) below.
(1)
I
corr
I
obs
=
10
( A
ex
+ A
em
) / 2

The equation (1) describes a basic correction procedure that has previously been implemented. Icorr and Iobs denotes the fluorescence intensity after and before IFE correction, respectively. Aem and Aex are the absorbance of the fluorophore and quencher, respectively. It should be noted that the simplified equation (1) can be used to correct the fluorescence spectrum taken with a 1 cm × 1 cm cuvette. Using equation (1), the fluorescence quenching intensities were corrected and plotted with respect to the various PA concentrations. The resultant Fig. 4 c unequivocally validated that the fluorescence quenching was mostly caused by the IFE mechanism. In order to further examine the IFE mechanism, the corrections were performed by considering the cell geometry and absorption characteristics of the aqueous solution of PDAB and PA with the Parker [46]equation (2).
(2)
F
cor
F
obsd
=
2.3 d A
ex
1 - 10
- d A
ex
10
gA
em
2.3 s A
em
1 - 10
- s A
em
where Fobsd is the observed fluorescence intensity, Fcor is the corrected fluorescence intensity, Aex and Aem are the absorbances at the maximum excitation and emission wavelengths, respectively, s is the thickness of the excitation beam (s = 0.10 cm), g is the distance between the edge of the excitation beam and the edge of cuvette (g = 0.40 cm), and d is the width of the cuvette (d = 1.00 cm). As shown in Fig. 4 d, we found that the suppressed efficiency of IFE, indicating that the suppressed efficiency mainly comes from IFE. Similar IFE-based sensors for picric acid detection have been reported previously [47–53].

In summary, the newly designed fluorescent probe PDAB was utilized successfully for the selective detection of PA. This PDAB probe exhibited excellent selectivity, functioning even in the presence of several interfering nitroaromatics, anions, and cations. Using the fluorimetric titration approach, the PDAB probe has a low detection limit for PA, down to 9.82 nM, demonstrating a greater detection sensitivity. The principal sensing mechanism was found to be IFE, which was confirmed by spectral overlap and unaltered fluorescence lifetime. Overall, this research work provides new insight into the development of highly sensitive ‘receptor-free’ fluorescent probes for the detection of picric acid.

Arunkumar Kathiravan: Conceptualization, Methodology, Data curation, Writing – original draft, Writing – review & editing. Mahalakshmi Narayanan: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Mariadoss Asha Jhonsi: Data curation, Writing – original draft. Venkattappan Anbazhagan: Conceptualization, Methodology.

ce:conflict-of-interest.ce:para: The authors declare that they have no known competing financial interests or personal relationships that could have appeared to influence the work reported in this paper.

ce:acknowledgment.ce:para: A.K. thanks to SERB Research Scientists scheme (SB/SRS/2018-2019-05/CS, Dated: 30/01/2019). A.K. also thanks the sophisticated analytical instruments facility (SAIF), Indian Institute of Technology, Chennai, for the fluorescence lifetime measurements. MAJ thanks the BSACIST for providing the seed grant (Ref. No. CSD/CSM/2022/08, dated: 14/07/2022) for conducting independent research. V.A. thanks Vinayaka Mission’s Research Foundation (Deemed to be University) for financial support (Research Grant No.: VMRF/SeedMoney-Phase2/2020-10/VMKVASC-Salem/1).

ce:section.ce:label: Appendix A
ce:section.ce:para: Supplementary data to this article can be found online at https://doi.org/10.1016/j.saa.2023.123166.

ce:section.ce:para:
The following are the Supplementary data to this article:
Supplementary data 1
